turns.content: I am getting this panic: task was aborted due to timeout; I thought I had enough time, but my main loop is starving. I tried using tokio::task::yield_now() in a compute block and it didn't help at all.

That panic happens because yield_now does not actually sleep the thread; it only tells the runtime to cooperate. If you are running on a single logical core, your long-running compute task will starve other tasks until you voluntarily give up control.
I understand now, but I still think yield_now should just pause execution like std::thread::sleep. Why isn't it working as I expected?
Exactly that is the misconception; yield_now does not block or sleep. It is a cooperative hint, so your code must call it explicitly between heavy operations to let other tasks run.
So if I wrap my heavy computation in an async fn and never call yield_now inside the loop, will tokio automatically insert pauses?
No. The runtime only yields when a task is ready to wait on something like a channel or timer; it does not auto-insert yield_now calls for pure compute.
Then how do I prevent starvation in my game loop? Should I just use tokio::time::sleep for every frame?
Sleeping is fine for timing, but for CPU-bound rendering or physics you should interleave yield_now with your calculations so the scheduler can switch contexts.
Can I use tokio::task::yield_now() in a regular sync function too?
Yes, yield_now works outside async blocks but it only has effect inside an active async task; ensure your game loop runs as a spawned task.
I tried putting yield_now inside my update function and the frame rate still dropped. What am I missing?
You must call yield_now frequently within the tight loop where you do heavy work; otherwise the scheduler sees your task as always ready and never switches.
Got it, I will add yield_now every N iterations. Is there a pattern or guideline for how often to call it?
There is no magic number; you should tune based on your CPU load and system cores—call it whenever the work takes more than a few microseconds per iteration.

Hey, I'm looking at my colleague's code and they're using a long-running computation inside an async block without any yield points. The function uses the task runtime but never calls anything that lets other tasks run. Is this actually okay for a simple script?

It might work in isolation, but in a real server or multi-user environment, that pattern can cause severe starvation. When a single task holds the runtime's scheduler exclusively while performing heavy CPU work, other requests are effectively blocked forever until that task finishes. The core issue is how cooperative multitasking relies on tasks voluntarily giving up control so others can proceed.

I see what you mean about blocking the scheduler, but isn't async just supposed to handle concurrency? If I'm doing a big calculation in an async function, shouldn't that be fine as long as it's inside the runtime?

That's a common misconception. The concept of cooperative multitasking means tasks must yield control back to the scheduler periodically; otherwise they starve others out. Even if your calculation is fast in isolation, on a CPU core with multiple active connections, that long-running operation will monopolize the thread pool and prevent other async tasks from making progress.
So I need to insert some kind of yield point inside my calculation? Like calling tokio::task::yield_now somewhere in the middle?

Exactly. You should call tokio::task::yield_now at strategic points within your heavy computation to let other pending tasks run their code. This prevents starvation and ensures fairness across all incoming requests.
Okay, I'll try adding yield_now calls. But wait, does this actually help with performance or is it just a workaround to avoid deadlock?

It's both. It avoids the starvation problem entirely by design, and in many cases improves overall throughput because the system can now process other requests while your task pauses briefly rather than waiting for it to finish before accepting more work.
